disagreements.title: Should you trust third-party intent data, or is first-party intent data fundamentally more reliable?

positions.stance: First-party intent signals are more reliable and should be weighted more heavily than third-party intent data. Third-party intent data is often low-quality, commoditized, and sold by multiple vendors under different names. Marketers should be deeply skeptical of third-party intent vendors and use first-party signals as the primary driver of targeting and budget allocation.

supporters.guest_context: B2B marketing practitioner building intent-based paid media systems
supporters.evidence: Richard explicitly prioritizes first-party signals (pricing page visits, guided tour interactions) over third-party intent data in account scoring, using third-party only as a secondary layer to expand reach. His results showed 3.5x average monthly pipeline and 5.2x pipeline from paid channels by weighting first-party signals.

supporters.guest_context: Head of Growth/Marketing at Navattic (interactive demo platform)
supporters.evidence: Natalie argues that first-party intent from interactive demo engagement is more accurate than third-party intent data because you know exactly what the prospect cared about, not just that they were 'in-market.' She recommends combining first-party data with any remaining third-party tools, implying first-party is the anchor.

Chris states most intent data in the market is low-quality and sold by multiple vendors under different names. He advises asking vendors whether they share/resell data and what constitutes a signal, arguing that most third-party intent data conflates intent-to-learn with intent-to-purchase, making it unreliable. He asserts only Google and Bing reliably capture true purchase intent.

Chris recommends vetting intent data providers on data sourcing and ownership, noting many providers purchase the same data from the same 1-2 sources, meaning competitors receive identical signals. He also advises placing accounts with weak/unproven intent into nurture rather than direct outreach, implying low confidence in third-party signals by default.

positions.stance: Third-party intent data, including from platforms like Bombora, 6sense, G2, and partner data platforms, provides valuable signals when layered with first-party data and industry-specific triggers. The key is using multiple intent sources together rather than relying on any single provider, and expanding beyond standard providers to find proprietary signals competitors aren't using.

supporters.guest_context: B2B demand generation and ABM practitioner
supporters.evidence: Jean recommends using third-party intent data (G2 reviews, competitor website visits, comparison pages) to identify accounts showing competitive intent and orchestrating dedicated automation workflows to reach them. No skepticism of third-party data quality is expressed; it is treated as actionable.

supporters.guest_context: B2B demand generation and ABM strategist
supporters.evidence: John recommends going beyond Bombora and 6sense to find industry-specific behavioral signals (job board activity, 10-K filings, real estate transactions), implying third-party providers are a valid baseline worth supplementing rather than replacing. The framing is additive, not dismissive.

supporters.guest_context: CEO of Sendoso (gifting and direct mail platform)
supporters.evidence: Kris recommends using partner data platforms (Crossbeam, Reveal) for third-party intent signals in acquisition and cross-sell, treating them as a complement to first-party and web intent data without expressing skepticism about data quality.

supporters.guest_context: B2B paid media and demand generation practitioner
supporters.evidence: Cindy recommends using tools like Clearbit and 6sense to identify behavioral intent signals (video presence, webinar activity, event participation) for ICP targeting in paid media, treating third-party enrichment tools as reliable inputs without qualification.

disagreements.context_dependency: Partially context-dependent: Chris Rack's skepticism is most applicable when evaluating commodity intent data vendors selling resold bitstream data. Jean Cameron and Kris Rudeegraap are referencing more specific third-party signals (G2 review activity, partner overlap data) that are arguably higher-quality than generic web traffic signals. However, the core disagreement — whether third-party intent data is trustworthy enough to act on directly versus requiring heavy vetting or replacement with first-party signals — is a genuine disagreement that applies broadly regardless of company stage.
disagreements.trend_note: The skepticism position (Chris Rack, episodes 140 and 150, mid-2024) predates the more pragmatic layering approach seen in later episodes (Jean Cameron, ep. 315, Dec 2025; Richard Meyer, ep. 341, Mar 2026). However, Richard Meyer in the most recent episode (341) still explicitly prioritizes first-party over third-party, so the skepticism has not fully given way to acceptance of third-party data.
disagreements.why_it_matters: Marketers allocating budget to intent data vendors need to know whether third-party signals are worth paying for or whether investing in first-party signal infrastructure (interactive demos, website visitor tracking, guided tours) will yield better ROI. Getting this wrong means either wasting budget on commoditized data or under-investing in proprietary signal capture.
